refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting a specific quality metric (packet loss). The refined FR isolates the quality/performance obligation while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing 'how' (e.g., redundancy, error correction, protocol selection) and focuses on the outcome: communication integrity under defined operational conditions. The phrase 'customer-defined packet loss limits' and 'agreed network conditions' preserves the acceptance criteria without embedding them as design constraints.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation trials under agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency profile, interference scenarios). Measure end-to-end packet loss across representative mission durations and worksite locations. Confirm measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%). Document network conditions, test duration, packet count, and loss rate.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall enable remote operation of equipment from a tele-operator station.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation command and feedback data transmission with packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The NGHE solution shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss, latency, signal loss) to the tele-operator and site manager in real time.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time loss detection and customer-configurable thresholds
DP2: Employ forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels tuned to measured network conditions
DP3: Establish dual independent communication paths with automatic failover and load balancing to reduce single-path loss impact
DP4: Deploy jitter buffering and packet reordering logic with timeout-based loss detection and on-demand retransmission
DP5: Implement network condition monitoring and dynamic codec/compression adjustment to reduce payload size and loss probability
DP6: Use a hybrid approach combining selective retransmission (for critical control frames) and FEC (for telemetry streams) with customer-tunable parameters
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP6: Implement a hybrid loss mitigation strategy that combines selective retransmission for critical control frames (operator commands, safety signals) with forward error correction for telemetry and status streams, supported by real-time network condition monitoring and customer-configurable loss thresholds and redundancy parameters.

potential_sources: Coupling between loss detection logic and retransmission scheduling—mitigated by separating detection (passive monitoring) from action (configurable retry policy)
Coupling between FEC encoder and packet serialization—mitigated by treating FEC as a transparent layer applied post-serialization
Coupling between network monitoring and codec selection—mitigated by making codec adjustment optional and decoupled from core loss mitigation
Coupling between customer configuration and runtime behavior—mitigated by externalizing thresholds and parameters to a configuration interface
coupling_check.notes: DP6 avoids tight coupling by separating concerns: detection (monitoring), decision (policy), and action (retransmission/FEC). Each concern can be independently tested and tuned. Dual-path failover (DP3) is orthogonal and can be composed with DP6 if needed. FEC and retransmission operate on different frame types, reducing interference.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-defined packet loss target and network conditions are measurable and stable during operation. Risk: If conditions are highly variable or unmeasurable, tuning parameters becomes difficult.

Assumption: Critical control frames can be reliably distinguished from telemetry. Risk: Misclassification could lead to over-protection of non-critical data or under-protection of safety-critical commands.

Assumption: Retransmission latency budget exists (i.e., operator can tolerate small delays for retry). Risk: If latency is extremely tight, retransmission may not be viable; FEC-only approach may be required.
Assumption: Network conditions are symmetric (loss in both directions). Risk: Asymmetric loss (e.g., downlink loss only) requires separate monitoring and mitigation strategies.
Assumption: Customer has capability to measure and report actual packet loss under agreed conditions. Risk: Lack of instrumentation or reporting discipline could prevent validation of the 1% target.

Assumption: Redundancy overhead (FEC parity packets, retransmission buffers) is acceptable within bandwidth and latency budgets. Risk: Excessive overhead could saturate the link or introduce unacceptable delay.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) acceptable retransmission latency budget (e.g., 'loss recovery must complete within X ms'); (2) definition of 'critical' vs. 'non-critical' frames for differential protection; (3) measurement methodology and instrumentation for validating the 1% target under agreed conditions; (4) expected network condition range (loss rate, jitter, bandwidth); (5) whether asymmetric loss scenarios must be handled. Additionally, clarify whether the 1% target applies to end-to-end application frames or raw link packets, as this affects DP design. Consider adding a secondary FR for 'The solution shall provide real-time visibility into network condition metrics and loss mitigation effectiveness' to support tuning and validation.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates hybrid loss mitigation strategy including selective retransmission logic for critical control frames, forward error correction for telemetry/status streams, real-time network condition monitoring, and customer-configurable loss thresholds and redundancy parameters.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and control frames (critical); selective retransmission logic applies to these frames

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits telemetry and status streams; forward error correction applied to these streams

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety signals (critical); selective retransmission logic applies to these frames

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Monitors real-time network conditions (latency, packet loss, bandwidth); feeds condition metrics to loss mitigation strategy; outputs encoded/retransmitted frames

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Teleoperation is the natural owner of operator command handling, safety signal processing, and telemetry transmission. Loss mitigation strategy directly supports reliable command delivery and status feedback. Network monitoring is localized to this module's communication layer. Customer-configurable parameters align with teleoperation service configuration. Minor diffusion: Network Link interface must be queried for condition metrics, but this is a clean dependency.

Partial fit: Safety Services owns safety signal generation and validation, but does not own operator command handling or telemetry transmission. Selective retransmission for safety signals could be localized here, but forward error correction for telemetry and loss threshold configuration would diffuse to Teleoperation. Hybrid strategy requires unified ownership across both critical and non-critical streams.

Poor fit: Vehicle module is responsible for vehicle state and actuation, not communication or network management. Loss mitigation strategy is fundamentally a communication-layer concern. Placing it in Vehicle would create tight coupling between vehicle dynamics and network protocol logic, violating separation of concerns.

Weak fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container. While it could theoretically host the strategy, it is too broad and would not provide clear ownership or interface boundaries. Loss mitigation is a specific communication service, not a system-wide orchestration concern. Placing it here would obscure responsibility and complicate testing/maintenance.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the most specific and cohesive boundary for DP6. It owns the full lifecycle of operator commands (Tasking in), safety signals (SafetyAlert in), and telemetry/status streams (Telemetry out). The hybrid loss mitigation strategy is a communication reliability service that directly supports these flows. Real-time network condition monitoring via the Network Link interface is a natural dependency for Teleoperation's communication layer. Customer-configurable loss thresholds and redundancy parameters are configuration concerns that belong with the service that uses them. Containment score of 0.92 reflects minimal diffusion: only the Network Link interface must be queried externally, which is a clean, well-defined dependency.
containment_risks: Network Link interface must provide real-time condition metrics (latency, packet loss, bandwidth) with sufficient granularity and update frequency for loss mitigation decisions. If Network Link does not expose these metrics, Teleoperation would need to infer them indirectly, increasing coupling.

Customer-configurable parameters (loss thresholds, redundancy levels) must be stored and retrieved within Teleoperation's scope. If configuration is managed by a separate system (e.g., a global configuration service), Teleoperation becomes dependent on that service, increasing diffusion.

Forward error correction and selective retransmission logic may require coordination with Vehicle module if vehicle state (e.g., motion state, battery level) influences redundancy decisions. This would create cross-module coupling.

Safety Services may need to validate that safety signals are retransmitted correctly. If Safety Services owns the validation logic, Teleoperation and Safety Services must coordinate, increasing coupling.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP6 is well-formulated and maps cleanly to Teleoperation. No revision is necessary. However, to minimize containment risks, ensure that: (1) Network Link interface explicitly exposes real-time network condition metrics as a query/subscription service; (2) Customer-configurable loss thresholds and redundancy parameters are stored as Teleoperation-owned configuration (not delegated to a global configuration service); (3) Forward error correction and retransmission logic are independent of vehicle state (i.e., loss mitigation is purely network-driven, not vehicle-state-driven); (4) Safety Services validates safety signal integrity post-retransmission via a clean interface (SafetyAlert out from Teleoperation, not tight coupling).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP6 directly addresses the FR requirement to maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits. The hybrid loss mitigation strategy (selective retransmission for critical frames + forward error correction for telemetry) is a sound logical solution. The DP explicitly includes real-time network condition monitoring and customer-configurable thresholds, which are necessary to operationalize the packet loss target (≤1% or customer-defined). The DP is sufficiently detailed to guide implementation without over-specifying the physical realization.

diffusion_risks: Network Link interface dependency: Teleoperation must query Network Link for real-time condition metrics (latency, packet loss, bandwidth). If Network Link does not expose these metrics as a well-defined, low-latency service, Teleoperation will be forced to infer conditions indirectly, increasing coupling and reducing decision accuracy.

Configuration ownership: Customer-configurable loss thresholds and redundancy parameters must be owned and stored within Teleoperation's scope. If a global configuration service manages these parameters, Teleoperation becomes dependent on that service for runtime updates, increasing diffusion and complicating testing.

Safety Services coordination: Safety signal retransmission logic resides in Teleoperation, but Safety Services owns safety signal validation. If Safety Services must validate retransmitted safety signals, a clean interface (SafetyAlert out from Teleoperation → SafetyAlert validation in Safety Services) is required. Tight coupling here would violate separation of concerns.

Vehicle state coupling: Forward error correction and retransmission decisions must remain network-driven and independent of vehicle state (motion, battery, actuation). If vehicle state influences redundancy levels, Teleoperation would need to query Vehicle module, creating cross-module coupling and increasing diffusion.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the correct ownership boundary with a containment score of 0.92. The module naturally owns operator command handling (Tasking in), safety signal processing (SafetyAlert in), and telemetry transmission (Telemetry out). The hybrid loss mitigation strategy is a communication reliability service that directly supports these flows. Real-time network condition monitoring via Network Link is a clean, well-defined external dependency. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and expected for a communication-layer service that must interact with the network substrate. The recommended revision addresses containment risks by ensuring Network Link exposes metrics explicitly, configuration is Teleoperation-owned, loss mitigation logic is network-driven (not vehicle-state-driven), and Safety Services validation occurs post-retransmission via a clean interface.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The Teleoperation module boundary is specific and measurable. Verification can be conducted via: (1) Network simulation tests with controlled packet loss rates (0%, 2%, 5%, 10%) to confirm that selective retransmission and forward error correction maintain effective packet loss ≤1% under agreed conditions; (2) Configuration tests to verify that customer-defined loss thresholds are correctly applied; (3) Interface tests to confirm that Network Link metrics are queried with sufficient granularity and update frequency; (4) Safety signal integrity tests to confirm that retransmitted safety signals are correctly validated by Safety Services. Acceptance criteria (packet loss ≤1% or customer-defined target) can be directly measured in simulation and field trials.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to the Teleoperation module with explicit responsibility for hybrid loss mitigation strategy, real-time network monitoring, and customer-configurable parameters. The external interfaces (Tasking, SafetyAlert, Telemetry, Network Link) are well-defined, and the direction of data flow is explicit. Ownership boundaries are unambiguous: Teleoperation owns loss mitigation logic; Safety Services owns safety signal validation; Vehicle owns vehicle state; Network Link owns network condition metrics. No allocation ambiguity or overlap exists.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly define the Network Link interface contract to expose real-time network condition metrics (latency, packet loss rate, bandwidth utilization) as a query or subscription service with specified update frequency (e.g., ≥10 Hz) and accuracy (e.g., ±5% for packet loss estimation). This ensures Teleoperation can make timely, accurate loss mitigation decisions.

Confirm that customer-configurable loss thresholds and redundancy parameters are stored and managed within Teleoperation's scope (e.g., as configuration files or a local configuration service), not delegated to a global configuration service. This minimizes external dependencies and simplifies testing.

Document that forward error correction and selective retransmission logic are purely network-driven (based on Network Link metrics) and independent of vehicle state. If vehicle state must influence redundancy decisions in the future, create an explicit Vehicle → Teleoperation interface rather than embedding vehicle state logic within Teleoperation.

Define the SafetyAlert validation interface: Teleoperation outputs retransmitted safety signals; Safety Services validates integrity post-retransmission. Document the validation protocol (e.g., checksum verification, sequence number validation) to ensure Safety Services can confirm that retransmitted signals are uncorrupted.

Add a test plan that verifies packet loss ≤1% (or customer-defined target) under agreed network conditions (e.g., 2% baseline packet loss, 50 ms latency, 10 Mbps bandwidth). Include both simulation and field trial scenarios to validate the hybrid loss mitigation strategy under realistic conditions.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence and clear allocation. However, the recommended revisions expose critical sub-requirements that must be decomposed to ensure architectural integrity and safety compliance. Specifically: (1) Network Link interface contract definition is a distinct FR requiring explicit DP and IM specification; (2) Configuration management (customer-defined thresholds) is a separate concern requiring its own FR-DP-IM chain; (3) Safety signal validation protocol is a safety-critical sub-requirement that must be decomposed to ensure Safety Services can verify retransmitted signals without ambiguity; (4) Vehicle state independence is a constraint that requires explicit sub-FR to prevent future coupling. High safety criticality demands that these sub-requirements be formally decomposed, verified, and traced rather than left as revision notes. Decomposition will also clarify the boundary between Teleoperation's loss mitigation logic and Safety Services' validation responsibility, reducing diffusion risk from 0.08 to near-zero.
next_level_sub_frs: FR6.1: Expose real-time network condition metrics (latency, packet loss rate, bandwidth utilization) to Teleoperation with specified update frequency (≥10 Hz) and accuracy (±5% for packet loss estimation).
FR6.2: Store and manage customer-configurable packet loss thresholds and redundancy parameters within Teleoperation's scope without external global configuration service dependency.
FR6.3: Ensure forward error correction and selective retransmission decisions are purely network-driven (based on Network Link metrics) and remain independent of vehicle state.
FR6.4: Validate integrity of retransmitted safety signals in Safety Services using a defined protocol (checksum verification, sequence number validation) to confirm uncorrupted transmission.
FR6.5: Verify packet loss ≤1% (or customer-defined target) under agreed network conditions (2% baseline packet loss, 50 ms latency, 10 Mbps bandwidth) via simulation and field trials.

revise.why: FR6 and DP6 are sound and require no revision at the current level. Revisions are addressed through sub-FR decomposition, not by reworking the parent FR-DP pair. The recommended revisions from the gate assessment are best captured as explicit sub-FRs to ensure traceability and verification.

assumptions: Network Link module will expose metrics as a well-defined, low-latency service (assumed in DP6; must be verified via FR6.1).
Customer-configurable thresholds can be stored locally within Teleoperation without requiring a global configuration service (assumed in DP6; must be verified via FR6.2).
Forward error correction and retransmission logic can remain independent of vehicle state without loss of safety or performance (assumed in DP6; must be verified via FR6.3).
Safety Services can validate retransmitted safety signals post-transmission using a well-defined protocol (assumed in DP6; must be verified via FR6.4).

Hybrid loss mitigation strategy (selective retransmission + FEC) is sufficient to achieve ≤1% effective packet loss under customer-defined network conditions (assumed in DP6; must be verified via FR6.5).
open_risks: Network Link interface contract may not expose metrics with sufficient granularity or update frequency; if so, Teleoperation will be forced to infer conditions indirectly, reducing decision accuracy and increasing coupling (Diffusion Risk: High). Mitigation: FR6.1 decomposition and explicit interface specification.

Customer-configurable thresholds may be managed by a global configuration service outside Teleoperation's scope; if so, Teleoperation becomes dependent on that service for runtime updates, increasing diffusion and complicating testing (Diffusion Risk: Medium). Mitigation: FR6.2 decomposition and ownership clarification.

Vehicle state may be required to influence redundancy decisions in future use cases (e.g., battery-aware retransmission); if so, Teleoperation would need to query Vehicle module, creating cross-module coupling (Diffusion Risk: Medium). Mitigation: FR6.3 decomposition and explicit constraint documentation.

Safety Services validation protocol may not be clearly defined; if so, retransmitted safety signals may be validated inconsistently or incompletely, creating safety gaps (Safety Risk: High). Mitigation: FR6.4 decomposition and protocol specification.

Hybrid loss mitigation strategy may be insufficient under extreme network conditions (e.g., >5% baseline packet loss); if so, effective packet loss may exceed 1% target (Performance Risk: Medium). Mitigation: FR6.5 decomposition and comprehensive testing under worst-case scenarios.
evidence_needed: Network Link interface specification document defining metric types, update frequency, accuracy, and query/subscription protocol.
Configuration management design document specifying where customer-defined thresholds are stored (local files, in-memory cache, database) and how they are updated at runtime.

Constraint documentation confirming that forward error correction and retransmission logic are network-driven and independent of vehicle state; if vehicle state must influence decisions, define explicit Vehicle → Teleoperation interface.
Safety signal validation protocol specification (checksum algorithm, sequence number validation rules, error handling) agreed between Teleoperation and Safety Services.
Test plan and results demonstrating packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions (2% baseline, 50 ms latency, 10 Mbps bandwidth) via simulation and field trials.
Traceability matrix linking FR6 → DP6 → IM (Teleoperation) → FR6.1-6.5 sub-FRs to ensure all recommended revisions are formally captured and verified.
